m_Item1.QuestionText: Which other keywords are also used to declare user defined type other than class?

Options.OptionText: struct
union
object
both a & b

m_Item1.QuestionText: Function overloading is also similar to which of the following?

Options.OptionText: operator overloading
constructor overloading
destructor overloading
none of the above

m_Item1.QuestionText: The characteristics of the static functions

Options.OptionText: It can't be declared const, volatile or virtual
It is always accessed with 'this' pointer
It can't directly access the non-static members of its class.
It must invoke through object of class.

m_Item1.Explanation:
Static Member Function in a class is the function that is declared as static because of which function attains certain properties as defined below:
A static member function is independent of any object of the class.
A static member function can be called even if no objects of the class exist.
A static member function can also be accessed using the class name through the scope resolution operator.
A static member function can access static data members and static member functions inside or outside of the class.
Static member functions have a scope inside the class and cannot access the current object pointer.
You can also use a static member function to determine how many objects of the class have been created.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is correct about function overloading?

Options.OptionText: The types of arguments are different
The order of argument is different.
The number of argument is same
Both A and B.

m_Item1.Explanation: Function overloading is multiple function with same name diffrent signiture.(number of argument,data type of argument,sequence of argument)

m_Item1.QuestionText: How many access specifiers can be  are present in  class?
m_Item1.Explanation: In CPP there are 3 Access specifiers.(private,public and protected).

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What will be the output of the program ?
#include<stdio.h>
int main()
{
float arr[] = {12.4, 2.3, 4.5, 6.7};
printf("%d ", sizeof(arr)/sizeof(arr[0]));
return 0;
}
m_Item1.Explanation: The program calculates the number of elements in the array arr by dividing the total size of arr by the size of a single element (float). Since there are four floats in the array (each typically 4 bytes on most systems), the output will be 4

m_Item1.QuestionText:
#include<iostream.h>
void main()
{
char str1[]=” India” , str2[]=” India” ;
if(str1==str2)
cout<<”Both the string are same” ;
else
cout<<”Both the string are not same” ;
}

Options.OptionText: Both the string are same
Both the string are not same
Compile Time error
Runtime Error

m_Item1.Explanation: use strcpy to to compare String .

m_Item1.QuestionText:
#include<iostream>
using namespace std;
int count=0;
class obj {
public :
obj()  {
count++;
}
~obj() {
count--;
}
};
int main()
{
obj A,B,C,D,E;
obj F;
{
obj G;
}
cout<<count;
return 0;
}
m_Item1.Explanation: When object is created counter will  increment and when object goes out of scope destructor will decrement  count. object G has local scope so counter will get increament and decrement . So total count is 6 only

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is static binding?

Options.OptionText: Binding of function call to the function definition done at the run time
Binding of function call to the function definition done at the compile time
Assigning values to static variables of a class
Defining static functions in a class to access static data

m_Item1.Explanation: Binding of function call to the function definition done at the compile time is static binding. Because runtime  binding is achived by virtual function.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What will be the output if the input provided is 5?
#include<iostream>
using namespace std;
int a = 1000;
class Test {
   int a;
   public:
  Test() {
     a = 0;
  }
  void get_a() {
    int a;
    cout << "Enter Value of A :";
    cin >> a;
  }
  void put_a() {
     cout << endl << a << " " << ::a;
  }
};
int main() {
   Test t1;
   t1.get_a();
   t1.put_a();
   return 0;
}

Options.OptionText: Compile time error

m_Item1.Explanation: t1 gets initialized with default constructor which sets a to 0. Function get_a() is called on t1 BUT it assigned the value entered by user to the local variable a (not the data member a of t1) so in put_a() a will show 0 wherese as ::a shows value of global variable a. Note that :: is an operator that can be used to access global data explicitly.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Constructors are used to

Options.OptionText: initalize the objects
construct the data members
both a & b
none of the mentioned

m_Item1.Explanation: A constructor is a member function of a class which initializes objects of a class. In C++, Constructor is automatically called when object(instance of class) create. It is special member function of the class.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Pick out the other definition of objects.

Options.OptionText: member of the class
associate of the class
attribute of the class
instance of the class

m_Item1.Explanation: Objects are instances of class, which holds the data variables declared in class and the member functions work on these class objects.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Pick out the correct statement about function overriding.

Options.OptionText: Defining many functions with same name but different parameters.
Re-defining a static function of base class with same name and parameters in the derived class.
Re-defining a non-virtual base class function with same name and parameters in the derived class.
Re-defining a virtual function of base class with same name and parameters in the derived class.

m_Item1.Explanation:
C++ Function Overriding. If derived class defines same function as defined in its base class, it is known as function overriding in C++. It is used to achieve runtime polymorphism. It enables you to provide derived class-specific implementation of the function which is already provided by its base class.
Function overriding is defining many functions with same name but different parameters: This is function overloading.
Re-defining a non-virtual function of base class with same name and parameters in the derived class: This is function hiding.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What does your class can hold?

Options.OptionText: data
functions
both a & b
none of the mentioned

m_Item1.Explanation: Class is container which holds data and functions both.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the correct way to define a class named 'Car' without any members in C++?

Options.OptionText: class Car { int speed; };
class Car { };
class Car { };;
class Car();

m_Item1.Explanation: In C++, the correct syntax to define a class with no members is to use curly braces without adding any data members or member functions. Option B is correct.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Given the class declaration: 'class Car { public: int speed; };', how do you create an object 'myCar' of this class?

Options.OptionText: myCar Car;
Car myCar{};
Car myCar();
Car myCar;

m_Item1.Explanation: The correct way to create an object in C++ is to declare it using the class name followed by the object name. Option D demonstrates this correctly.

m_Item1.QuestionText: If you have a class 'Car' as follows: 'class Car { public: int speed; };', how do you access the 'speed' attribute of an object 'myCar'?

Options.OptionText: myCar->speed;
myCar.speed;
Car.speed;
speed.myCar;

m_Item1.Explanation: When accessing a public member of an object, the dot operator (.) is used. Option B correctly demonstrates how to access the 'speed' attribute.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following best describes a constructor in C++?

Options.OptionText: A special member function that initializes objects.
A function that destroys objects.
A function that must always return a value.
A function called manually to initialize values.

m_Item1.Explanation: A constructor is a special member function with the same name as the class, used for initializing objects of that class. Option A describes it accurately.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What will occur if a constructor is not defined for a class?

Options.OptionText: The class cannot have any objects created.
The compiler provides a default constructor.
All members must be explicitly initialized.
The class will generate a compile-time error.

m_Item1.Explanation: If no constructor is defined, C++ automatically provides a default constructor that does nothing but allows object creation. Hence, Option B is correct.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is a class in C++?

Options.OptionText: An instance of a blueprint for objects.
A type of variable to store integers.
A blueprint to create functions only.
A blueprint for creating objects.

m_Item1.Explanation: In C++, a class serves as a blueprint for creating objects by defining attributes and behaviors. Option D correctly describes a class.

m_Item1.QuestionText: How can you define multiple objects of the same class using a single declaration?

Options.OptionText: Car myCar1, myCar2;
Car: myCar1, myCar2;
Car myCar1; Car myCar2;
Car myCar1 myCar2;

m_Item1.Explanation: In C++, you can define multiple objects of the same class in a single statement by separating them with commas. Thus, Option A is correct.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which operator is used to access members of a class using a pointer to an object?
m_Item1.Explanation: The arrow operator (->) is used to access members of a class when dealing with pointers to objects. Option C correctly identifies this.

m_Item1.QuestionText: How is a destructor identified within a class?

Options.OptionText: It has the same name as the class but prefixed with a tilde (~).
It has a different name and is prefixed with a tilde (~).
It is called the same as a constructor.
It must have return type void.

m_Item1.Explanation: A destructor in C++ is identified by having the same name as the class with a tilde (~) prefix and is used for cleanup. Therefore, Option A is correct.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the purpose of private access specifier in a class?

Options.OptionText: To make class members visible only within the class.
To provide global access to class members.
To make class members visible only to derived classes.
To make class members visible to all functions in the program.

m_Item1.Explanation: In C++, the private access specifier restricts access to class members by making them accessible only within the class itself. Hence, Option A is correct.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following statements is true regarding constructors in C++?

Options.OptionText: Constructors do not have a return type.
Constructors can be virtual.
Constructors can be overloaded.
Constructors can be explicitly inherited.

m_Item1.Explanation: Constructors do not have a return type. They are special member functions invoked to initialize objects.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is NOT a characteristic of a destructor in C++?

Options.OptionText: It can be overloaded.
It has no return type.
It is called when an object is destroyed.
It has the same name as the class prefixed with a tilde (~).

m_Item1.Explanation: Destructors cannot be overloaded; a class can have only one destructor.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the main purpose of a destructor in C++?

Options.OptionText: To allocate resources.
To release resources.
To copy an object.
To initialize an object.

m_Item1.Explanation: A destructor is used to release resources that the object may have acquired during its lifetime, such as memory or file handles.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following scenarios would require a custom destructor in C++?

Options.OptionText: Class with only static methods.
Class that allocates memory dynamically.
Class with only private members.
Class with inline functions only.

m_Item1.Explanation: A class that allocates memory dynamically requires a destructor to ensure that the memory is properly deallocated to avoid memory leaks.

m_Item1.QuestionText: If you do not explicitly define a destructor in your C++ class, what happens?

Options.OptionText: No destructor is provided.
A default destructor is provided by the compiler.
The program will not compile.
A runtime error will occur when the object is destroyed.

m_Item1.Explanation: If no destructor is provided, the compiler generates a default destructor that performs default destruction of all member objects.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is true about a copy constructor in C++?

Options.OptionText: It is used to initialize an object using another object of the same class.
It must return a pointer to the class object.
It cannot be used to initialize constant objects.
It requires a return type of void.

m_Item1.Explanation: A copy constructor initializes an object using another object of the same class, typically for creating a copy of that object.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which keyword is used to prevent a class from being inherited in C++?

Options.OptionText: protected
private
final
sealed

m_Item1.Explanation: The keyword 'final' is used in C++ to prevent a class from being inherited.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the order of calling constructors and destructors in a class hierarchy?

Options.OptionText: Destructor of derived class, destructor of base class, constructor of derived class, constructor of base class.
Constructor of base class, constructor of derived class, destructor of derived class, destructor of base class.
Constructor of derived class, constructor of base class, destructor of base class, destructor of derived class.
Destructor of base class, destructor of derived class, constructor of base class, constructor of derived class.

m_Item1.Explanation: In a class hierarchy, the constructor of the base class is called first, then the constructor of the derived class. Upon destruction, the destructor of the derived class is called, followed by the destructor of the base class.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is an example of a syntax error in constructor definition?

Options.OptionText: Rectangle();
~Rectangle();
Rectangle() const;
Rectangle& Rectangle();

m_Item1.Explanation: A constructor cannot be declared as const because it is meant to initialize members and potentially modify them.

m_Item1.QuestionText: When are destructors called in a C++ program?

Options.OptionText: Only when the program ends.
When an object goes out of scope or is explicitly deleted.
Only when the user calls them explicitly.
Only in multithreaded applications.

m_Item1.Explanation: Destructors are called automatically when an object goes out of scope or when the delete operator is applied to a pointer to an object.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following will prevent a destructor from running?

Options.OptionText: Declaring it as private.
Making it virtual.
Throwing an exception in the destructor.
Deleting the object using delete.

m_Item1.Explanation: Declaring a destructor as private prevents it from being called outside the class context, thus potentially preventing its execution.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following options is true regarding the use of virtual destructors?

Options.OptionText: Virtual destructors are not recommended in C++.
Virtual destructors ensure that the destructor of a derived class is called when deleting a base class pointer.
Virtual destructors slow down the program significantly and should be avoided.
Virtual destructors are used to make objects non-copyable.

m_Item1.Explanation: If a class is intended to be inherited and used polymorphically, declaring a destructor as virtual ensures that the destructor for derived classes is called when a base class pointer is deleted.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following scenarios require a copy constructor?

Options.OptionText: Passing an object by reference to a function.
Assigning one object to another of the same class.
Initializing an object with another object of the same class.
Using an object with only static members.

m_Item1.Explanation: A copy constructor is needed when you initialize (or copy) an object using another object of the same class.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following correctly describes the default constructor?

Options.OptionText: A constructor that takes no arguments and initializes an object with default values.
A constructor that takes multiple arguments.
A constructor which needs to be explicitly defined in every class.
A constructor used only for static data members.

m_Item1.Explanation: A default constructor is a constructor that takes no arguments and is used to create objects of a class with default values.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following actions would not automatically invoke a copy constructor in C++?

Options.OptionText: Returning an object from a function.
Passing an object by value to a function.
Initializing an object with another object.
Assigning an object using the assignment operator.

m_Item1.Explanation: Using the assignment operator does not invoke the copy constructor; it calls the assignment operator overload if defined, or the default assignment if not.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the primary purpose of a copy constructor in C++?

Options.OptionText: Initialize an object using another object of the same class.
Allocate memory for an array.
Provide default values to all member variables.
Define a new class object.

m_Item1.Explanation: A copy constructor in C++ is used to create a new object as a copy of an existing object of the same class.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is an example of a correct copy constructor declaration in C++?

Options.OptionText: MyClass(MyClass &obj);
MyClass(MyClass obj);
MyClass MyClass(MyClass &obj);
void MyClass(MyClass obj);

m_Item1.Explanation: A correct copy constructor must take a reference (usually non-const) to an object of the same class.

m_Item1.QuestionText: When is a copy constructor called in C++?

Options.OptionText: When an object is assigned to another object.
When an object is initialized with another object of the same class.
When an object is deleted.
When an object is converted from one class to another.

m_Item1.Explanation: A copy constructor is called when a new object is initialized using another object of the same class.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which scenario may invoke a copy constructor in C++?

Options.OptionText: Passing an object by value to a function.
Returning an object from a function by reference.
Assigning one object to another.
Calling the destructor of an object.

m_Item1.Explanation: Passing an object by value to a function creates a copy of the object, invoking the copy constructor.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following scenarios is NOT a typical use case for a copy constructor?

Options.OptionText: Initialization using a temporary object.
Creating copies of an object.
Deep copying dynamic memory in an object.
Releasing resources held by an object.

m_Item1.Explanation: Releasing resources is typically the job of a destructor, not a copy constructor.

m_Item1.QuestionText: How does a copy constructor differ from an assignment operator in C++?

Options.OptionText: A copy constructor creates a new object, while an assignment operator modifies an existing object.
A copy constructor modifies an existing object, while an assignment operator creates a new object.
Both perform the same function.
An assignment operator initializes a new object.

m_Item1.Explanation: The copy constructor creates a new object initialized with an existing object, while the assignment operator updates an existing object with values from another object.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What happens if no copy constructor is defined in a class?

Options.OptionText: The C++ compiler provides a default copy constructor.
The class cannot create any objects.
The program will not compile.
The assignment operator is used instead.

m_Item1.Explanation: If no copy constructor is defined, the C++ compiler automatically provides a default copy constructor.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Why might you define your own copy constructor in C++?

Options.OptionText: To handle deep copying or manage resource allocation.
To prevent the creation of any objects.
To handle the layout of data members in memory.
To improve the readability of code.

m_Item1.Explanation: A user-defined copy constructor is typically necessary for deep copying, especially when dealing with pointers to dynamically allocated memory.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is true about the copy constructor and static objects?

Options.OptionText: A copy constructor is called whenever a static object is declared.
Static objects do not call copy constructors.
A copy constructor can be called when a static object is initialized with another object.
Static objects always use assignment operators.

m_Item1.Explanation: When a static object is initialized with another object of the same class, the copy constructor is invoked.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which option best describes a shallow copy?

Options.OptionText: Copying all fields directly without any modification.
Copying an object with deep clones of its pointed data.
Copying only the primitive data members.
Copying an object and allocating new resources for all pointers.

m_Item1.Explanation: A shallow copy involves copying an object by replicating its fields directly, which means pointers still point to the same memory location as the original.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the primary purpose of the scope resolution operator (::) in C++?

Options.OptionText: To access global variables when there is a local variable with the same name.
To provide access to class members.
To define a new scope within functions.
To allocate memory for objects dynamically.

m_Item1.Explanation: The scope resolution operator is used to access global variables when there is a conflict with local variables of the same name.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In which of the following scenarios is the scope resolution operator used with class identifiers?

Options.OptionText: When defining methods outside the class definition.
To initialize member variables.
For overloading operators.
To declare friend functions.

m_Item1.Explanation: The scope resolution operator can be used to define methods that are declared inside the class body but defined outside of it, by specifying the class to which the functions belong.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following statements correctly demonstrates the use of the scope resolution operator for accessing a class static member?

Options.OptionText: ObjectName::staticMember;
className->staticMember;
className::staticMember;
ObjectName->staticMember;

m_Item1.Explanation: Class static members should be accessed using the scope resolution operator (::), prefixed by the class name.

m_Item1.QuestionText: When using nested namespaces, how is the scope resolution operator applied?

Options.OptionText: By separating the namespace names with ::, such as namespace1::namespace2::variable.
By using standard library imports.
By defining all namespaces globally.
By using the 'using' keyword with all namespace hierarchies.

m_Item1.Explanation: The scope resolution operator can be used to specify nested namespaces by chaining them with ::.

m_Item1.QuestionText: How can the scope resolution operator (::) be used to call a base class function from a derived class which has overridden this function?

Options.OptionText: BaseClassName::functionName();
DerivedClassName::functionName();
Super::functionName();
this->BaseClassName::functionName();

m_Item1.Explanation: To call the base class version of a function that has been overridden in the derived class, use the base class name followed by the scope resolution operator and the function name.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What does it mean when the scope resolution operator is used without a class or namespace before it?

Options.OptionText: It refers to the global version of a variable or function.
It increases the function's access level to public.
It acts as a pointer dereferencing tool.
It initializes the variable to a default value.

m_Item1.Explanation: Using the scope resolution operator with no prefix accesses global variables or functions, assuming they are not locally overridden.

m_Item1.QuestionText: How is the scope resolution operator utilized in the context of enumerations?

Options.OptionText: To define a scope for all enumeration values globally.
To access specific enumerators from a scoped enumeration.
To iterate through enumeration values.
To avoid naming conflicts between different enumerations.

m_Item1.Explanation: For scoped enums (enum class), the scope resolution operator is used to access specific enumerators.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following correctly uses the scope resolution operator for accessing a nested class within a superclass?

Options.OptionText: NestedClass::Superclass::method();
Superclass::NestedClass::method();
Superclass.NestedClass::method();
Superclass->NestedClass->method();

m_Item1.Explanation: In C++, the correct way to access a nested class is by specifying the superclass followed by :: and then the nested class name and the member access.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In the context of C++ templates, where is the scope resolution operator used?

Options.OptionText: To define template parameters globally.
To specify template parameters in instantiation.
To distinguish template member functions from non-template member functions.
To access type names inside template classes.

m_Item1.Explanation: When a member function is defined outside the template class, the scope resolution operator is used to specify type names from a nested template.

m_Item1.QuestionText: If a function is declared both globally and within a class, how would you invoke the global function within the class?

Options.OptionText: ::functionName();
this->functionName();
className::functionName();
global::functionName();

m_Item1.Explanation: To call a globally declared function within a class if there's a name conflict, use the scope resolution operator with the global declaration, like ::functionName().
